exercises.title: De Morgan's Law – Basic NOT AND
exercises.question: Apply De Morgan's Law to simplify: ¬(A ∧ B)
exercises.hint: De Morgan's Law states: ¬(A ∧ B) = ¬A ∨ ¬B. The AND becomes OR, and each variable is negated.
exercises.explanation: By De Morgan's First Law, the complement of a conjunction is the disjunction of the complements: ¬(A ∧ B) = ¬A ∨ ¬B.

exercises.title: De Morgan's Law – Basic NOT OR
exercises.question: Apply De Morgan's Law to simplify: ¬(A ∨ B)
exercises.hint: De Morgan's Law states: ¬(A ∨ B) = ¬A ∧ ¬B. The OR becomes AND, and each variable is negated.
exercises.explanation: By De Morgan's Second Law, the complement of a disjunction is the conjunction of the complements: ¬(A ∨ B) = ¬A ∧ ¬B.

exercises.title: De Morgan's Law – Three Variables (AND)
exercises.question: Apply De Morgan's Law to: ¬(A ∧ B ∧ C)
acceptableAnswers: NOT A OR NOT B OR NOT C
exercises.hint: De Morgan's Law extends to multiple variables. ¬(A ∧ B ∧ C) negates each variable and changes AND to OR.
exercises.explanation: De Morgan's Law generalises to n variables: ¬(A ∧ B ∧ C) = ¬A ∨ ¬B ∨ ¬C.

exercises.title: De Morgan's Law – Three Variables (OR)
exercises.question: Apply De Morgan's Law to: ¬(A ∨ B ∨ C)
acceptableAnswers: NOT A AND NOT B AND NOT C
exercises.hint: De Morgan's Law extends to multiple variables. ¬(A ∨ B ∨ C) negates each variable and changes OR to AND.
exercises.explanation: De Morgan's Law generalises: ¬(A ∨ B ∨ C) = ¬A ∧ ¬B ∧ ¬C.

exercises.title: De Morgan's with Nested Expression
exercises.question: Simplify using De Morgan's Law: ¬(A ∧ (B ∨ C))
acceptableAnswers: NOT A OR (NOT B AND NOT C)
exercises.hint: First apply De Morgan's to the outer ¬AND, then apply De Morgan's again to the inner ¬(B ∨ C).
exercises.explanation: First: ¬(A ∧ (B ∨ C)) = ¬A ∨ ¬(B ∨ C). Then apply De Morgan's again: ¬(B ∨ C) = ¬B ∧ ¬C. Result: ¬A ∨ (¬B ∧ ¬C).

exercises.title: De Morgan's with Nested OR
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬((A ∨ B) ∧ C)
acceptableAnswers: (NOT A AND NOT B) OR NOT C
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's to the outer AND first, then simplify the ¬(A ∨ B) using De Morgan's again.

exercises.title: De Morgan's – Double Application
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(¬(A) ∧ B)
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's: ¬(X ∧ Y) = ¬X ∨ ¬Y. Then ¬(¬A) simplifies to A by double negation.
exercises.explanation: ¬(¬A ∧ B) = ¬(¬A) ∨ ¬B = A ∨ ¬B (using double negation: ¬¬A = A).

exercises.title: De Morgan's – Double Application (OR)
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(A ∨ ¬(B))
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's: ¬(X ∨ Y) = ¬X ∧ ¬Y. Then ¬(¬B) = B.

exercises.title: De Morgan's – Complex Nested
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬((A ∧ B) ∨ (C ∧ D))
acceptableAnswers: (NOT A OR NOT B) AND (NOT C OR NOT D)
exercises.hint: First apply De Morgan's to the outer OR, then apply De Morgan's to each inner AND.

exercises.title: De Morgan's – Complex with Complement
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(¬(A ∨ B) ∧ ¬(C ∨ D))
acceptableAnswers: A OR B OR C OR D
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's to the outer AND, then use double negation to remove the double NOTs.

exercises.title: De Morgan's – Verify Equivalence
exercises.question: Show that ¬(A ∧ B) ∨ ¬(C ∧ D) is equivalent to a simpler form. Simplify: ¬((A ∧ B) ∧ (C ∧ D))
acceptableAnswers: NOT A OR NOT B OR NOT C OR NOT D
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's directly to a four-variable AND expression.

exercises.title: Identify the Law
exercises.question: Which Boolean law is demonstrated by the equivalence: ¬(P ∨ Q) = ¬P ∧ ¬Q?
exercises.hint: This law shows how a NOT distributes over an OR by changing it to AND and negating each term.
exercises.explanation: This is De Morgan's Second Law: the complement of a disjunction equals the conjunction of the complements.

exercises.title: De Morgan's – Mixed Operations
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(A ∧ B) ∧ ¬(A ∨ B)
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's to both terms separately. Then look for absorption.
exercises.explanation: ¬(A∧B) ∧ ¬(A∨B) = (¬A∨¬B) ∧ (¬A∧¬B). Since (¬A∧¬B) implies (¬A∨¬B), by absorption this simplifies to ¬A∧¬B.

exercises.title: De Morgan's – Reverse Application
exercises.question: Express using a single NOT: (¬A) ∨ (¬B)
exercises.hint: De Morgan's Law can be applied in reverse. ¬A ∨ ¬B can be written as ¬(? ? ?).
exercises.explanation: By De Morgan's First Law in reverse: ¬A ∨ ¬B = ¬(A ∧ B).

exercises.title: De Morgan's – Reverse Application (AND)
exercises.question: Express using a single NOT: (¬A) ∧ (¬B)
exercises.hint: De Morgan's Law can be applied in reverse. ¬A ∧ ¬B can be written as ¬(? ? ?).
exercises.explanation: By De Morgan's Second Law in reverse: ¬A ∧ ¬B = ¬(A ∨ B).

exercises.title: Distributive Law – AND over OR
exercises.question: Expand using the Distributive Law: A ∧ (B ∨ C)
exercises.hint: The Distributive Law works like expanding brackets in algebra: A(B+C) = AB + AC.
exercises.explanation: A ∧ (B ∨ C) = (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ C). This is analogous to algebraic expansion.

exercises.title: Distributive Law – OR over AND
exercises.question: Expand: A ∨ (B ∧ C)
exercises.hint: In Boolean algebra, OR also distributes over AND: A + BC = (A+B)(A+C).
exercises.explanation: A ∨ (B ∧ C) = (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ C). Unlike normal algebra, OR distributes over AND in Boolean algebra.

exercises.title: Distributive Law – Factoring
exercises.question: Factorise: (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ C)
exercises.hint: Look for a common factor in both terms. What variable appears in both (A ∧ B) and (A ∧ C)?
exercises.explanation: (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ C) = A ∧ (B ∨ C). Factor out the common variable A.

exercises.title: Distributive Law – Three Terms
exercises.question: Factorise: (X ∧ Y) ∨ (X ∧ Z) ∨ (X ∧ W)
acceptableAnswers: X AND (Y OR Z OR W)
exercises.hint: X is common to all three terms. Factor it out.

exercises.title: Distributive + Complement
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∧ (¬A ∨ B)
exercises.hint: Distribute A over the OR, then use the Complement Law: A ∧ ¬A = 0.

exercises.title: Distributive – Expand and Simplify
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ ¬B)
exercises.hint: Use the Distributive Law (OR over AND) in reverse, then apply the Complement Law.

exercises.title: Distributive – Two Different Factors
exercises.question: Expand: (A ∨ B) ∧ (C ∨ D)
exercises.hint: Distribute each term in the first bracket over each term in the second bracket, like FOIL in algebra.

exercises.title: Distributive – Simplify After Expansion
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ B) ∧ (¬A ∨ B)
exercises.hint: Factor out B using the Distributive Law (OR over AND) in reverse.

exercises.title: Distributive – Factor with Complement
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ (¬A ∧ B)
exercises.hint: Factor out B from both terms.

exercises.title: Distributive – Complex Factoring
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B ∧ C) ∨ (A ∧ B ∧ ¬C)
exercises.hint: Factor out the common A ∧ B from both terms.

exercises.title: Distributive Law – Right-side Factor
exercises.question: Expand using the Distributive Law: (B ∨ C) ∧ A
exercises.hint: By commutativity, (B ∨ C) ∧ A = A ∧ (B ∨ C). Now distribute.

exercises.title: Distributive – Consensus Theorem Setup
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ (¬A ∧ C) ∨ (B ∧ C)
acceptableAnswers: (A AND B) OR (NOT A AND C)
exercises.hint: The third term (B ∧ C) is redundant. This is the Consensus Theorem: AB + A'C + BC = AB + A'C.
exercises.explanation: B∧C = B∧C∧(A∨¬A) = (A∧B∧C) ∨ (¬A∧B∧C). The first part is absorbed by A∧B and the second by ¬A∧C. So (B∧C) is redundant.

exercises.title: Absorption Law – Basic (OR)
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ (A ∧ B)
exercises.hint: The Absorption Law states: A ∨ (A ∧ B) = A. The second term is 'absorbed' by the first.
exercises.explanation: A ∨ (A ∧ B) = A. If A is true, the whole expression is true. If A is false, both terms are false.

exercises.title: Absorption Law – Basic (AND)
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∧ (A ∨ B)
exercises.hint: The Absorption Law states: A ∧ (A ∨ B) = A.
exercises.explanation: A ∧ (A ∨ B) = A. This is the dual form of the Absorption Law.

exercises.title: Absorption Law – Reversed Order
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ A
exercises.hint: By commutativity of OR, this is the same as A ∨ (A ∧ B). Apply Absorption.

exercises.title: Absorption Law – With Complement
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ (¬A ∧ B)
exercises.hint: This is a variant of absorption. Distribute to get (A ∨ ¬A) ∧ (A ∨ B), then simplify.

exercises.title: Absorption – Three Variables
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ (A ∧ B ∧ C)
exercises.hint: The Absorption Law works regardless of how many variables are ANDed with A.
exercises.explanation: A ∨ (A ∧ B ∧ C) = A. A absorbs any conjunction that includes A.

exercises.title: Absorption – Recognise Pattern
exercises.question: Simplify: (X ∧ Y) ∨ (X ∧ Y ∧ Z)
exercises.hint: X ∧ Y absorbs X ∧ Y ∧ Z, since the second term is a subset of the first.
exercises.explanation: (X∧Y) ∨ (X∧Y∧Z) = X∧Y. The more specific term is absorbed by the more general one.

exercises.title: Absorption – Hidden Pattern
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ B ∧ ¬C) ∨ (A ∧ B ∧ C)
exercises.hint: The first term A ∧ B absorbs both other terms since they are more specific.
exercises.explanation: Both (A∧B∧¬C) and (A∧B∧C) are absorbed by (A∧B). Result: A∧B.

exercises.title: Absorption – AND Form
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ B ∨ C)
exercises.hint: By the dual Absorption Law: X ∧ (X ∨ Y) = X. Here X = A ∨ B.
exercises.explanation: (A∨B) ∧ (A∨B∨C) = A∨B. The more restrictive clause absorbs the less restrictive one.

exercises.title: Absorption + Complement Simplification
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ (¬A ∧ B) ∨ (¬A ∧ ¬B)
exercises.hint: Factor ¬A from the last two terms, then simplify.

exercises.title: Identify Absorption Law
exercises.question: Which law simplifies A ∨ (A ∧ B) to A?
exercises.hint: This law is named after the idea that one term 'absorbs' the other.
exercises.explanation: The Absorption Law: A ∨ (A ∧ B) = A and its dual A ∧ (A ∨ B) = A.

exercises.title: Identity Law – OR with 0
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ 0
exercises.hint: 0 is the identity element for OR. ORing with 0 doesn't change the value.
exercises.explanation: A ∨ 0 = A. Zero is the identity element for the OR operation.

exercises.title: Identity Law – AND with 1
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∧ 1
exercises.hint: 1 is the identity element for AND. ANDing with 1 doesn't change the value.
exercises.explanation: A ∧ 1 = A. One is the identity element for the AND operation.

exercises.title: Null Law – OR with 1
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ 1
exercises.hint: Anything ORed with 1 gives 1. This is the Null (Domination) Law for OR.
exercises.explanation: A ∨ 1 = 1. One is the annihilator for the OR operation.

exercises.title: Null Law – AND with 0
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∧ 0
exercises.hint: Anything ANDed with 0 gives 0. This is the Null (Domination) Law for AND.
exercises.explanation: A ∧ 0 = 0. Zero is the annihilator for the AND operation.

exercises.title: Idempotent Law – OR
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ A
exercises.hint: ORing a variable with itself gives the same variable. This is the Idempotent Law.
exercises.explanation: A ∨ A = A. This is the Idempotent Law for OR.

exercises.title: Idempotent Law – AND
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∧ A
exercises.hint: ANDing a variable with itself gives the same variable. This is the Idempotent Law.
exercises.explanation: A ∧ A = A. This is the Idempotent Law for AND.

exercises.title: Combined Identity Laws
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ 1) ∨ (B ∧ 0)
exercises.hint: Apply Identity Law to the first term and Null Law to the second term.

exercises.title: Idempotent with Extra Terms
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ A ∨ B
exercises.hint: First apply the Idempotent Law to A ∨ A.
exercises.explanation: A ∨ A ∨ B = A ∨ B (by Idempotent Law: A ∨ A = A).

exercises.title: Null Law in Context
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ 1) ∧ B
exercises.hint: First simplify A ∨ 1 using the Null Law, then apply the Identity Law.

exercises.title: Identify the Law – Idempotent
exercises.question: Which law is demonstrated by: A ∧ A ∧ A = A?
exercises.hint: This law says that performing the same operation with the same value repeatedly gives the same result.
exercises.explanation: The Idempotent Law: A ∧ A = A and A ∨ A = A. It extends to any number of repetitions.

exercises.title: Commutative Law – AND
exercises.question: Rewrite using the Commutative Law: B ∧ A
exercises.hint: The Commutative Law says the order doesn't matter for AND and OR.
exercises.explanation: B ∧ A = A ∧ B. The order of operands doesn't affect the result.

exercises.title: Commutative Law – OR
exercises.question: Rewrite using the Commutative Law: C ∨ A
exercises.hint: The Commutative Law says the order doesn't matter.
exercises.explanation: C ∨ A = A ∨ C.

exercises.title: Associative Law – AND
exercises.question: Rewrite using the Associative Law: (A ∧ B) ∧ C
exercises.hint: The Associative Law says you can change the grouping of AND or OR.
exercises.explanation: (A ∧ B) ∧ C = A ∧ (B ∧ C). Grouping doesn't affect the result.

exercises.title: Associative Law – OR
exercises.question: Rewrite using the Associative Law: (A ∨ B) ∨ C
exercises.hint: The Associative Law says you can change the grouping.

exercises.title: Reorder to Simplify
exercises.question: Use Commutative and Associative laws to simplify: B ∧ A ∧ ¬B
exercises.hint: Rearrange to group B ∧ ¬B together, then apply the Complement Law.

exercises.title: Reorder to Simplify (OR)
exercises.question: Use Commutative and Associative laws to simplify: B ∨ A ∨ ¬B
exercises.hint: Rearrange to group B ∨ ¬B together.

exercises.title: Identify – Associative vs Commutative
exercises.question: Which law is demonstrated by: (A ∨ B) ∨ C = A ∨ (B ∨ C)?
exercises.hint: This law is about changing the grouping (brackets), not the order.
exercises.explanation: The Associative Law allows regrouping: (A ∨ B) ∨ C = A ∨ (B ∨ C).

exercises.title: Identify – Commutative Law
exercises.question: Which law is demonstrated by: A ∧ B = B ∧ A?
exercises.hint: This law is about changing the order of operands.
exercises.explanation: The Commutative Law: A ∧ B = B ∧ A. The order of operands doesn't matter.

exercises.title: Complement Law – AND
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∧ ¬A
exercises.hint: A variable ANDed with its complement always gives 0 (false).
exercises.explanation: A ∧ ¬A = 0. A variable cannot be both true and false simultaneously.

exercises.title: Complement Law – OR
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ ¬A
exercises.hint: A variable ORed with its complement always gives 1 (true).
exercises.explanation: A ∨ ¬A = 1. A variable is always either true or false.

exercises.title: Complement in Expression
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ ¬A) ∨ B
exercises.hint: First simplify A ∧ ¬A using the Complement Law, then apply the Identity Law.

exercises.title: Complement – Both Forms
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ ¬A) ∧ (B ∧ ¬B)
exercises.hint: Simplify each bracket using the Complement Law, then combine.

exercises.title: Complement with Multiple Variables
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∧ (¬A ∨ ¬B)
exercises.hint: Recognise that (¬A ∨ ¬B) = ¬(A ∧ B) by De Morgan's Law.

exercises.title: Complement – Hidden
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∧ (¬A ∨ B) ∧ (¬A ∨ ¬B)
exercises.hint: First simplify A ∧ (¬A ∨ B) using distribution, then continue.
exercises.explanation: A ∧ (¬A ∨ B) = A∧B. Then A∧B ∧ (¬A ∨ ¬B) = A∧B ∧ ¬(A∧B) = 0.

exercises.title: Complement – OR Form Hidden
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ B) ∨ (¬A ∧ ¬B)
exercises.hint: Recognise that (¬A ∧ ¬B) = ¬(A ∨ B) by De Morgan's Law.

exercises.title: NOT of Constants
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬1
exercises.hint: The complement of 1 (true) is 0 (false).
exercises.explanation: ¬1 = 0. The complement of true is false.

exercises.title: NOT of Zero
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬0
exercises.hint: The complement of 0 (false) is 1 (true).
exercises.explanation: ¬0 = 1. The complement of false is true.

exercises.title: Complement – Complex Expression
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B ∧ C) ∨ (A ∧ B ∧ ¬C) ∨ (¬A)
exercises.hint: Factor (A ∧ B) from the first two terms, use Complement Law, then simplify with ¬A.
exercises.explanation: (A∧B∧C) ∨ (A∧B∧¬C) ∨ ¬A = A∧B∧(C∨¬C) ∨ ¬A = A∧B ∨ ¬A = ¬A ∨ B (by absorption variant).

exercises.title: Double Negation – Basic
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(¬A)
exercises.hint: Two NOTs cancel each other out.
exercises.explanation: ¬(¬A) = A. The double negation (involution) law.

exercises.title: Double Negation – In Expression
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(¬A) ∧ B
exercises.hint: First simplify ¬(¬A) using double negation.

exercises.title: Triple Negation
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(¬(¬A))
exercises.hint: Apply double negation from the inside out, or note that three NOTs = one NOT.
exercises.explanation: ¬(¬(¬A)) = ¬A. An odd number of negations gives a single negation.

exercises.title: Double Negation + De Morgan's
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(¬(A ∧ B))
exercises.hint: Double negation cancels out, regardless of what's inside.
exercises.explanation: ¬(¬(A ∧ B)) = A ∧ B. The double negation cancels.

exercises.title: Quadruple Negation
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(¬(¬(¬A)))
exercises.hint: Four NOTs cancel out (even number of negations).
exercises.explanation: ¬(¬(¬(¬A))) = ¬(¬A) = A. An even number of negations cancels completely.

exercises.title: Multi-step – Factor and Reduce
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ ¬B) ∨ (¬A ∧ B)
exercises.hint: Factor A from the first two terms to get A, then combine with the third term.
exercises.explanation: (A∧B) ∨ (A∧¬B) = A∧(B∨¬B) = A. Then A ∨ (¬A∧B) = A ∨ B.

exercises.title: Multi-step – De Morgan's + Simplify
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(A ∨ B) ∨ A
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's first, then simplify using Absorption variant.
exercises.explanation: ¬(A∨B) ∨ A = (¬A∧¬B) ∨ A = A ∨ ¬B (by absorption variant).

exercises.title: Multi-step – Expand then Reduce
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∧ (A ∨ B ∨ C)
exercises.hint: This is a direct application of the Absorption Law.
exercises.explanation: A ∧ (A ∨ B ∨ C) = A (by Absorption).

exercises.title: Multi-step – Four Minterms
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ ¬B) ∨ (¬A ∧ B) ∨ (¬A ∧ ¬B)
exercises.hint: Factor: first two terms give A, last two give ¬A. Then A ∨ ¬A = ?

exercises.title: Multi-step – Complex Expression
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ ¬B)
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's to the first term, then simplify.
exercises.explanation: ¬(A∧B) ∨ (A∧¬B) = (¬A∨¬B) ∨ (A∧¬B) = ¬A ∨ ¬B (since A∧¬B is absorbed by ¬B).

exercises.title: Multi-step – Triple Variable Simplification
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ C) ∧ (B ∨ C)
acceptableAnswers: (A AND B) OR (A AND C) OR (B AND C)
exercises.hint: First expand (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ C) using distribution, then AND with (B ∨ C).

exercises.title: Multi-step – Redundant Terms
exercises.question: Simplify: A ∨ (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ C)
exercises.hint: Apply Absorption: A absorbs any term that includes A.
exercises.explanation: A absorbs (A∧B) and (A∧C). Result: A.

exercises.title: Multi-step – Complex with De Morgan's
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬(¬A ∧ ¬B) ∧ ¬(¬A ∧ B)
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's to each ¬(...) term, then simplify.

exercises.title: Multi-step – Working Backwards
exercises.question: Simplify: (¬A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ B)
exercises.hint: Factor out B using the Distributive Law (OR over AND) in reverse.

exercises.title: Multi-step – Five-Term Simplification
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ A ∨ (A ∧ C) ∨ (B ∧ A)
exercises.hint: All other terms contain A and are absorbed by the standalone A.
exercises.explanation: A absorbs (A∧B), (A∧C), and (B∧A). (A∧B) and (B∧A) are the same by commutativity. Result: A.

exercises.title: Multi-step – NOT Distribution
exercises.question: Simplify: ¬((A ∨ B) ∧ (C ∨ D)) ∧ (A ∨ B)
acceptableAnswers: (A OR B) AND NOT C AND NOT D
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's to the NOT term, then simplify using absorption/complement.

exercises.title: Multi-step – Repeated Variable
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ B) ∧ A
exercises.hint: This is the Absorption Law: A ∧ (A ∨ B) = A.

exercises.title: Multi-step – XOR Expansion
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ ¬B) ∨ (¬A ∧ B)
acceptableAnswers: (A AND NOT B) OR (NOT A AND B)
exercises.hint: This expression is the definition of XOR (exclusive OR). It cannot be further simplified using basic Boolean laws.
exercises.explanation: (A ∧ ¬B) ∨ (¬A ∧ B) = A ⊕ B. This is the canonical form of XOR.

exercises.title: Multi-step – Challenging Reduction
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∨ B ∨ C) ∧ (A ∨ ¬B) ∧ (A ∨ ¬C)
exercises.hint: Start by combining the last two terms: (A ∨ ¬B) ∧ (A ∨ ¬C) = A ∨ (¬B ∧ ¬C).

exercises.title: Multi-step – Identity Clean-up
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ 1) ∨ (0 ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ B)
exercises.hint: Apply Identity and Null laws to the first two terms, then use Absorption.

exercises.title: CIE Style – Simplify Expression
exercises.question: Simplify the following Boolean expression, showing each law used: A ∧ (A ∨ B)
exercises.hint: This is a direct application of the Absorption Law.
exercises.explanation: A ∧ (A ∨ B) = A [Absorption Law]. In CIE exams, you must name the law used at each step.

exercises.title: CIE Style – Two-Step Simplification
exercises.question: Simplify, naming the law at each step: ¬(¬A ∨ B) ∨ B
exercises.hint: Step 1: Apply De Morgan's to ¬(¬A ∨ B). Step 2: Simplify using known laws.
exercises.explanation: Step 1: ¬(¬A ∨ B) = A ∧ ¬B [De Morgan's + Double Negation]. Step 2: (A ∧ ¬B) ∨ B = A ∨ B [Absorption variant].

exercises.title: CIE Style – Three-Step Simplification
exercises.question: Simplify, showing all working: ¬(A ∧ B) ∧ (A ∨ B)
acceptableAnswers: (A AND NOT B) OR (NOT A AND B)
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's, then distribute. The result is the XOR function.

exercises.title: CIE Style – Truth Table Verification
exercises.question: Verify using a truth table that ¬(A ∧ B) = ¬A ∨ ¬B. What are the output values for the four input combinations (A=0,B=0), (A=0,B=1), (A=1,B=0), (A=1,B=1)?
exercises.lawUsed: De Morgan's First Law (Truth Table Proof)
exercises.explanation: For all four input combinations, ¬(A ∧ B) and ¬A ∨ ¬B produce identical outputs: 1, 1, 1, 0. This verifies De Morgan's First Law.

exercises.title: CIE Style – SOP to Simplified
exercises.question: Simplify the Sum of Products: A'BC + AB'C + ABC' + ABC
acceptableAnswers: (A AND B) OR (A AND C) OR (B AND C)
exercises.hint: Group terms that differ by one variable. ABC pairs with each of the other three terms.
exercises.explanation: Pair ABC with A'BC: BC(A∨¬A) = BC. Pair ABC with AB'C: AC(B∨¬B) = AC. Pair ABC with ABC': AB(C∨¬C) = AB. Result: AB ∨ AC ∨ BC.

exercises.title: CIE Style – Named Law Application
exercises.question: State which Boolean law simplifies the expression A ∨ (¬A ∧ B) to A ∨ B.
exercises.hint: This is a well-known simplification pattern. Think about how A 'absorbs' part of the second term.
exercises.explanation: This is the complement variant of the Absorption Law. Proof: A ∨ (¬A∧B) = (A∨¬A)∧(A∨B) = 1∧(A∨B) = A∨B.

exercises.title: CIE Style – Logic Gate to Expression
exercises.question: A logic circuit has inputs A, B, C. The output X = ¬((A ∧ B) ∨ C). Simplify ¬((A ∧ B) ∨ C).
acceptableAnswers: (NOT A OR NOT B) AND NOT C
exercises.hint: Apply De Morgan's to the outer ¬OR, then apply De Morgan's to ¬(A ∧ B).
exercises.explanation: ¬((A∧B)∨C) = ¬(A∧B) ∧ ¬C [De Morgan's] = (¬A∨¬B) ∧ ¬C [De Morgan's].

exercises.title: CIE Style – Prove Equivalence
exercises.question: Prove algebraically that ¬(A) ∧ (A ∨ B) = ¬(A) ∧ B
exercises.hint: Distribute ¬A over the OR, then use the Complement Law.
exercises.explanation: ¬A ∧ (A ∨ B) = (¬A ∧ A) ∨ (¬A ∧ B) [Distributive] = 0 ∨ (¬A ∧ B) [Complement] = ¬A ∧ B [Identity].

exercises.title: CIE Style – Redundancy Elimination
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ B ∧ C) ∨ B
exercises.hint: B absorbs both (A ∧ B) and (A ∧ B ∧ C).
exercises.explanation: B absorbs (A∧B) and (A∧B∧C) since both require B to be true. Result: B.

exercises.title: CIE Style – NAND Expression
exercises.question: Express using only NAND: The expression ¬(A) can be written as A NAND A. Write (¬A) ∧ (¬B) using NAND notation.
acceptableAnswers: NOT((A NAND A) NAND (B NAND B)) NAND NOT((A NAND A) NAND (B NAND B))
¬((A NAND A) NAND (B NAND B)) NAND ¬((A NAND A) NAND (B NAND B))
exercises.hint: ¬A = A NAND A. Then X ∧ Y = ¬(X NAND Y) = (X NAND Y) NAND (X NAND Y). But more directly, De Morgan's gives ¬A∧¬B = ¬(A∨B). Think step by step.
exercises.explanation: ¬A = A↑A. ¬B = B↑B. ¬A∧¬B: Let P=A↑A, Q=B↑B. P∧Q = ¬(P↑Q) — but we need this as NAND. Actually (A↑A)↑(B↑B) = ¬(¬A∧¬B) = A∨B. So we need ¬(A∨B). Hmm, the direct NAND implementation of ¬A∧¬B = ((A↑A)↑(B↑B))↑((A↑A)↑(B↑B)).

exercises.title: CIE Style – Product of Sums
exercises.question: Convert to Sum of Products form: (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ ¬B)
exercises.hint: Factor out A using the Distributive Law (OR over AND), then use the Complement Law.

exercises.title: CIE Style – Full Simplification Chain
exercises.question: Simplify fully, showing each step: ¬(A ∨ B) ∨ (A ∧ B) ∨ A
exercises.hint: A absorbs (A ∧ B). Then simplify ¬(A ∨ B) ∨ A using De Morgan's and absorption.
exercises.explanation: Step 1: A absorbs (A∧B): expression becomes ¬(A∨B) ∨ A. Step 2: (¬A∧¬B) ∨ A = A ∨ ¬B [absorption variant].

exercises.title: CIE Style – Exam Question Format
exercises.question: A processor uses the Boolean expression X = (A ∧ ¬B) ∨ (¬A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ B) to control a flag. Simplify X.
exercises.hint: Group the last two terms: (¬A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ B) = B. Then simplify (A ∧ ¬B) ∨ B.
exercises.explanation: (¬A∧B) ∨ (A∧B) = B∧(¬A∨A) = B. Then (A∧¬B) ∨ B = A ∨ B.

exercises.title: CIE Style – Four Variable
exercises.question: Simplify: (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ C) ∨ (¬A ∧ B ∧ C)
acceptableAnswers: (A AND B) OR (A AND C) OR (B AND C)
exercises.hint: Note that ¬A∧B∧C can be rewritten. Add and subtract A∧B∧C to create a useful pattern.
exercises.explanation: Actually (A∧B)∨(A∧C)∨(¬A∧B∧C). Since A∧B∧C is absorbed by both A∧B and A∧C, we can add it: expression = (A∧B)∨(A∧C)∨(A∧B∧C)∨(¬A∧B∧C) = (A∧B)∨(A∧C)∨(B∧C).

exercises.title: CIE Style – Final Challenge
exercises.question: Simplify completely: ¬((¬A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ ¬B))
acceptableAnswers: (A AND NOT B) OR (NOT A AND B)
exercises.hint: First expand the inner expression, then apply De Morgan's. The result is a well-known function.
exercises.explanation: (¬A∨B)∧(A∨¬B) is XNOR (equivalence). Its complement is XOR. So ¬((¬A∨B)∧(A∨¬B)) = (A∧¬B)∨(¬A∧B) = A⊕B.
